content.text:
# Agent Stuff

This repository contains skills and extensions that I use across projects. Note that I often fine-tune these for specific repos, so some items may need small adjustments before reuse.

It is released on npm as `mitsupi` for use with the [Pi](https://buildwithpi.ai/) package loader.

## Skills

All skills live in the [`skills`](https://github.com/mitsuhiko/agent-stuff/blob/main/skills) folder:

-   [`/anachb`](https://github.com/mitsuhiko/agent-stuff/blob/main/skills/anachb) - Query Austrian public transport (VOR AnachB) for departures, routes, and disruptions.
-   [`/apple-mail`](https://github.com/mitsuhiko/agent-stuff/blob/main/skills/apple-mail) - Search/read Apple Mail local storage and extract attachments.
-   [`/commit`](https://github.com/mitsuhiko/agent-stuff/blob/main/skills/commit) - Create git commits using concise Conventional Commits-style subjects.
-   [`/frontend-design`](https://github.com/mitsuhiko/agent-stuff/blob/main/skills/frontend-design) - Design and implement distinctive frontend interfaces.
-   [`/ghidra`](https://github.com/mitsuhiko/agent-stuff/blob/main/skills/ghidra) - Reverse engineer binaries using Ghidra's headless analyzer.
-   [`/github`](https://github.com/mitsuhiko/agent-stuff/blob/main/skills/github) - Interact with GitHub using the `gh` CLI (issues, PRs, runs, APIs).
-   [`/google-workspace`](https://github.com/mitsuhiko/agent-stuff/blob/main/skills/google-workspace) - Access Google Workspace APIs via local helper scripts.
-   [`/librarian`](https://github.com/mitsuhiko/agent-stuff/blob/main/skills/librarian) - Cache and refresh remote git repositories in `~/.cache/checkouts`.
-   [`/mermaid`](https://github.com/mitsuhiko/agent-stuff/blob/main/skills/mermaid) - Create and validate Mermaid diagrams with Mermaid CLI tooling.
-   [`/native-web-search`](https://github.com/mitsuhiko/agent-stuff/blob/main/skills/native-web-search) - Trigger native web search with concise summaries and source URLs.
-   [`/oebb-scotty`](https://github.com/mitsuhiko/agent-stuff/blob/main/skills/oebb-scotty) - Plan Austrian rail journeys via ÖBB Scotty API.
-   [`/openscad`](https://github.com/mitsuhiko/agent-stuff/blob/main/skills/openscad) - Create/render OpenSCAD models and export STL files.
-   [`/pi-share`](https://github.com/mitsuhiko/agent-stuff/blob/main/skills/pi-share) - Load and parse session transcripts from shittycodingagent.ai/buildwithpi/pi.dev URLs.
-   [`/sentry`](https://github.com/mitsuhiko/agent-stuff/blob/main/skills/sentry) - Fetch and analyze Sentry issues, events, transactions, and logs.
-   [`/summarize`](https://github.com/mitsuhiko/agent-stuff/blob/main/skills/summarize) - Convert files/URLs to Markdown via `uvx markitdown` and summarize.
-   [`/tmux`](https://github.com/mitsuhiko/agent-stuff/blob/main/skills/tmux) - Drive tmux sessions via keystrokes and pane output scraping.
-   [`/update-changelog`](https://github.com/mitsuhiko/agent-stuff/blob/main/skills/update-changelog) - Update changelogs with notable user-facing changes.
-   [`/uv`](https://github.com/mitsuhiko/agent-stuff/blob/main/skills/uv) - Use `uv` for Python dependency management and script execution.
-   [`/web-browser`](https://github.com/mitsuhiko/agent-stuff/blob/main/skills/web-browser) - Browser automation via Chrome/Chromium CDP.

## Pi Coding Agent Extensions

Custom extensions for Pi Coding Agent are in [`pi-extensions`](https://github.com/mitsuhiko/agent-stuff/blob/main/pi-extensions):

-   [`answer.ts`](https://github.com/mitsuhiko/agent-stuff/blob/main/pi-extensions/answer.ts) - Interactive TUI for answering questions one by one.
-   [`btw.ts`](https://github.com/mitsuhiko/agent-stuff/blob/main/pi-extensions/btw.ts) - Simple `/btw` side-chat popover with optional summary injection back into the main chat on close.
-   [`context.ts`](https://github.com/mitsuhiko/agent-stuff/blob/main/pi-extensions/context.ts) - Context breakdown (extensions, skills, AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md) + token usage, including loaded-skill highlighting.
-   [`control.ts`](https://github.com/mitsuhiko/agent-stuff/blob/main/pi-extensions/control.ts) - Session control helpers (list controllable sessions, etc.).
-   [`files.ts`](https://github.com/mitsuhiko/agent-stuff/blob/main/pi-extensions/files.ts) - Unified file browser with git status + session references and reveal/open/edit/diff actions.
-   [`split-fork.ts`](https://github.com/mitsuhiko/agent-stuff/blob/main/pi-extensions/split-fork.ts) - `/split-fork` command to branch the current session into a new pi process in a right-hand Ghostty split.
-   [`go-to-bed.ts`](https://github.com/mitsuhiko/agent-stuff/blob/main/pi-extensions/go-to-bed.ts) - Late-night safety guard with explicit confirmation after midnight.
-   [`loop.ts`](https://github.com/mitsuhiko/agent-stuff/blob/main/pi-extensions/loop.ts) - Prompt loop for rapid iterative coding with optional auto-continue.
-   [`multi-edit.ts`](https://github.com/mitsuhiko/agent-stuff/blob/main/pi-extensions/multi-edit.ts) - Replaces the built-in `edit` tool with batch `multi` edits and Codex-style `patch` support, including preflight validation.
-   [`notify.ts`](https://github.com/mitsuhiko/agent-stuff/blob/main/pi-extensions/notify.ts) - Native desktop notifications when the agent finishes.
-   [`prompt-editor.ts`](https://github.com/mitsuhiko/agent-stuff/blob/main/pi-extensions/prompt-editor.ts) - In-editor prompt mode selector with persistence, history, config, and shortcuts.
-   [`review.ts`](https://github.com/mitsuhiko/agent-stuff/blob/main/pi-extensions/review.ts) - Code review command (working tree, PR-style diff, commits, custom instructions, optional fix loop).
-   [`session-breakdown.ts`](https://github.com/mitsuhiko/agent-stuff/blob/main/pi-extensions/session-breakdown.ts) - TUI for 7/30/90-day session and cost analysis with usage graph.
-   [`todos.ts`](https://github.com/mitsuhiko/agent-stuff/blob/main/pi-extensions/todos.ts) - Todo manager extension with file-backed storage and TUI.
-   [`uv.ts`](https://github.com/mitsuhiko/agent-stuff/blob/main/pi-extensions/uv.ts) - Helpers for uv-based Python workflows.
-   [`whimsical.ts`](https://github.com/mitsuhiko/agent-stuff/blob/main/pi-extensions/whimsical.ts) - Replaces the default thinking message with random whimsical phrases.

## Pi Coding Agent Themes

Custom themes are in [`pi-themes`](https://github.com/mitsuhiko/agent-stuff/blob/main/pi-themes):

-   [`nightowl.json`](https://github.com/mitsuhiko/agent-stuff/blob/main/pi-themes/nightowl.json) - Night Owl-inspired theme.

## Plumbing Commands

These command files need customization before use. They live in [`plumbing-commands`](https://github.com/mitsuhiko/agent-stuff/blob/main/plumbing-commands):

-   [`/make-release`](https://github.com/mitsuhiko/agent-stuff/blob/main/plumbing-commands/make-release.md) - Automates repository release with version management.

## Intercepted Commands

Command wrappers live in [`intercepted-commands`](https://github.com/mitsuhiko/agent-stuff/blob/main/intercepted-commands):

-   [`pip`](https://github.com/mitsuhiko/agent-stuff/blob/main/intercepted-commands/pip)
-   [`pip3`](https://github.com/mitsuhiko/agent-stuff/blob/main/intercepted-commands/pip3)
-   [`poetry`](https://github.com/mitsuhiko/agent-stuff/blob/main/intercepted-commands/poetry)
-   [`python`](https://github.com/mitsuhiko/agent-stuff/blob/main/intercepted-commands/python)
-   [`python3`](https://github.com/mitsuhiko/agent-stuff/blob/main/intercepted-commands/python3)

## Summary of `mitsuhiko/agent-stuff`

This is **Armin Ronacher's** (mitsuhiko) personal collection of **skills, extensions, and themes** for AI coding agents — primarily targeting the **[Pi coding agent](https://buildwithpi.ai/)**. It's published on npm as `mitsupi` for easy installation via Pi's package loader.

### What's inside

| Category | Count | Highlights |
|---|---|---|
| **Skills** | 20 | Reusable agent instructions for specific tasks — git commits, GitHub CLI, Sentry, tmux, web browsing (CDP), Ghidra reverse engineering, OpenSCAD, Austrian transit APIs, Google Workspace, and more |
| **Pi Extensions** | 17 | TypeScript plugins that extend Pi's TUI — a side-chat (`/btw`), session cost analysis, code review with fix loops, a late-night "go to bed" guard, desktop notifications, whimsical thinking messages, batch multi-edit tool, prompt loop for rapid iteration, etc. |
| **Pi Themes** | 1 | A Night Owl-inspired color theme |
| **Plumbing Commands** | 1 | An automated release workflow with version management |
| **Intercepted Commands** | 5 | Wrappers for `pip`, `pip3`, `poetry`, `python`, `python3` (likely redirecting to `uv`-based equivalents) |

### Key takeaways

- **Opinionated & personal** — tuned for Armin's workflow; may need adjustments for reuse.
- **Pi-centric** — most value targets the Pi coding agent ecosystem (skills, extensions, themes).
- **Broad scope** — ranges from everyday dev tasks (git, GitHub, changelogs) to niche tools (Austrian rail planner, Ghidra RE, Apple Mail scraping).
- **Notable extensions** — `go-to-bed.ts` (blocks coding past midnight 😄), `session-breakdown.ts` (cost/usage analytics), `split-fork.ts` (Ghostty terminal splits), and `multi-edit.ts` (replaces the built-in edit with batch + patch support).

content.thinking: The user wants me to compare the commit skill from mitsuhiko/agent-stuff with the one in this dotfiles project. Let me first find our commit skill/instructions, and also fetch their commit skill from GitHub.

content.text: Let me fetch both commit skills.

---
name: commit
description: "Read this skill before making git commits"
---

Create a git commit for the current changes using a concise Conventional Commits-style subject.

## Format

`<type>(<scope>): <summary>`

- `type` REQUIRED. Use `feat` for new features, `fix` for bug fixes. Other common types: `docs`, `refactor`, `chore`, `test`, `perf`.
- `scope` OPTIONAL. Short noun in parentheses for the affected area (e.g., `api`, `parser`, `ui`).
- `summary` REQUIRED. Short, imperative, <= 72 chars, no trailing period.

## Notes

- Body is OPTIONAL. If needed, add a blank line after the subject and write short paragraphs.
- Do NOT include breaking-change markers or footers.
- Do NOT add sign-offs (no `Signed-off-by`).
- Only commit; do NOT push.
- If it is unclear whether a file should be included, ask the user which files to commit.
- Treat any caller-provided arguments as additional commit guidance. Common patterns:
  - Freeform instructions should influence scope, summary, and body.
  - File paths or globs should limit which files to commit. If files are specified, only stage/commit those unless the user explicitly asks otherwise.
  - If arguments combine files and instructions, honor both.

## Steps

1. Infer from the prompt if the user provided specific file paths/globs and/or additional instructions.
2. Review `git status` and `git diff` to understand the current changes (limit to argument-specified files if provided).
3. (Optional) Run `git log -n 50 --pretty=format:%s` to see commonly used scopes.
4. If there are ambiguous extra files, ask the user for clarification before committing.
5. Stage only the intended files (all changes if no files specified).
6. Run `git commit -m "<subject>"` (and `-m "<body>"` if needed).

---
name: commit
description: Create one-line commits without co-authored-by
---

# Commit

Create a git commit with a one-line message, without the co-authored-by footer.

## Usage

```
/commit
/commit fix typo in readme
```

If `$ARGUMENTS` is provided, use it as guidance for the commit message.

## Instructions

1. Run `git status` to see staged and unstaged changes
2. Run `git diff --cached` to see what will be committed (if files are staged)
3. Run `git diff` to see unstaged changes (if nothing is staged yet)
4. If no files are staged, ask the user which files to stage or stage all relevant changes
5. Analyze the changes to determine if they should be split into separate commits:
   - If changes are logically distinct (e.g., a bug fix AND a new feature, or changes to unrelated modules), split them into separate commits
   - Each commit should represent a single logical change
   - Stage and commit each logical group separately
6. Create a concise one-line commit message that:
   - Follows conventional commits format (feat:, fix:, chore:, docs:, refactor:, test:, etc.)
   - Summarizes the changes clearly
   - Is under 72 characters if possible
7. Commit with: `git commit -m "message"`

Do NOT include a co-authored-by footer.

ENOENT: no such file or directory, access '/Users/thxgg/Projects/Personal/dotfiles/.pi/agent/AGENTS.md'

# Global Agent Instructions

## Git Commits

When making git commits, follow these rules:

1. Inspect `git status`, staged changes, unstaged changes, and recent commit messages before committing.
2. Stage only the relevant files if they are not already staged.
3. Split logically distinct work into separate commits when needed.
4. Use [Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/) format: `<type>(<scope>): <summary>`.
5. Include a scope when it adds clarity; omit it for root-level or obvious changes.
6. Prefer a project-local domain scope rather than the repo name.
7. Keep the message under 72 characters when practical.
8. Do not include a co-authored-by footer.
9. Do not include ticket IDs or story references in commit messages unless the project conventions say otherwise.

Here's a side-by-side comparison:

## Commit Skill Comparison

| Aspect | **Ours** (`common/.config/agents/skills/commit/SKILL.md` + global `AGENTS.md`) | **mitsuhiko's** |
|---|---|---|
| **Format** | Conventional Commits (`type: summary`) | Conventional Commits (`type(scope): summary`) |
| **Scope** | Global AGENTS.md says "include scope when it adds clarity"; skill itself doesn't mention scope | Explicitly defines scope as optional with examples (`api`, `parser`, `ui`) |
| **72-char limit** | ✅ "under 72 characters if possible" | ✅ "<= 72 chars" |
| **Body** | Not mentioned (one-line only) | Explicitly optional — "add a blank line after the subject and write short paragraphs" |
| **Co-authored-by** | Explicitly forbidden (both in skill + AGENTS.md) | Not mentioned |
| **Sign-offs** | Not mentioned | Explicitly forbidden (`Signed-off-by`) |
| **Breaking changes** | Not mentioned | Explicitly forbidden (no `!` marker or footers) |
| **Push after commit** | Not mentioned | Explicitly says "do NOT push" |
| **File arguments** | Not handled — asks user if nothing staged | Rich handling: file paths/globs limit scope; freeform text guides the message; ambiguous files prompt clarification |
| **Commit splitting** | ✅ Actively splits logically distinct changes into separate commits | ❌ Not mentioned — seems to assume one commit |
| **Log inspection** | Global AGENTS.md says inspect recent commits | Optional step: `git log -n 50 --pretty=format:%s` to discover common scopes |
| **Workflow steps** | 7 steps, more exploratory (status → diff → stage → analyze → split → commit) | 6 steps, more structured (infer args → status/diff → optional log → clarify → stage → commit) |
| **`$ARGUMENTS` handling** | Basic: "use it as guidance for the commit message" | Detailed: distinguishes file paths/globs vs. freeform instructions vs. combined |

### Key differences

1. **Theirs is more defensive** — explicitly bans push, breaking-change markers, sign-offs, and footers. Ours focuses mainly on banning co-authored-by.

2. **Theirs handles arguments better** — parses `/commit src/api fix auth bug` into file filters + message guidance, whereas ours treats arguments as a simple hint.

3. **Ours splits commits; theirs doesn't** — our skill actively analyzes whether changes should be broken into multiple logical commits, which is a significant advantage.

4. **Theirs supports commit bodies** — allows multi-paragraph descriptions when needed, while ours is strictly one-line.

5. **Our commit rules are split across two files** — the skill handles mechanics, while global `AGENTS.md` adds scope conventions and log inspection. Theirs is self-contained in one file.

### Suggestions to improve ours
- Add explicit "do not push" guard
- Add argument parsing (file paths vs. instructions)
- Allow optional body for complex changes
- Add the `git log` step to discover common scopes
- Consolidate the split rules into the skill itself so it's self-contained

# Global Agent Instructions

## Git Commits

When making git commits, follow these rules:

1. Inspect `git status`, staged changes, unstaged changes, and recent commit messages before committing.
2. Stage only the relevant files if they are not already staged.
3. Split logically distinct work into separate commits when needed.
4. Use [Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/) format: `<type>(<scope>): <summary>`.
5. Include a scope when it adds clarity; omit it for root-level or obvious changes.
6. Prefer a project-local domain scope rather than the repo name.
7. Keep the message under 72 characters when practical.
8. Do not include a co-authored-by footer.
9. Do not include ticket IDs or story references in commit messages unless the project conventions say otherwise.

Before I edit, here's my suggestion on the workflow steps:

**Split the list into two sections: workflow (what to do) and format (how to write the message).** Right now the 9 rules mix process steps (1–3) with formatting rules (4–9), which makes it harder to follow. Two sections keeps each concern scannable and makes the ordering of steps explicit.

Proposed structure:

- **Workflow** — inspect status/diff → inspect log for scopes → stage → split if needed → commit → don't push
- **Format** — type/scope/summary spec, body allowance, footer restrictions

The content stays the same, just reorganized. Want me to go ahead with this structure?

arguments.newText:
## Git Commits

### Workflow

1. Run `git status` and `git diff` (staged + unstaged) to understand current changes.
2. Run `git log -n 50 --pretty=format:%s` to check recent message style and discover common scopes.
3. Stage only the relevant files if they are not already staged.
4. If changes are logically distinct, split them into separate commits.
5. Commit with `git commit -m "<subject>"` (and `-m "<body>"` if a body is needed).
6. Do not push.

### Format

- Use [Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/): `<type>(<scope>): <summary>`.
- `type` is required: `feat`, `fix`, `docs`, `refactor`, `chore`, `test`, `perf`, etc.
- `scope` is optional. Include when it adds clarity; prefer project-local domain scopes over the repo name.
- `summary`: imperative mood, ≤ 72 characters, no trailing period.
- Body is optional. If needed, add a blank line after the subject and write short paragraphs.
- Do not include a co-authored-by footer.
- Do not include ticket IDs or story references unless project conventions say otherwise.

# Global Agent Instructions

## Git Commits

### Workflow

1. Run `git status` and `git diff` (staged + unstaged) to understand current changes.
2. Run `git log -n 50 --pretty=format:%s` to check recent message style and discover common scopes.
3. Stage only the relevant files if they are not already staged.
4. If changes are logically distinct, split them into separate commits.
5. Commit with `git commit -m "<subject>"` (and `-m "<body>"` if a body is needed).
6. Do not push.

### Format

- Use [Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/): `<type>(<scope>): <summary>`.
- `type` is required: `feat`, `fix`, `docs`, `refactor`, `chore`, `test`, `perf`, etc.
- `scope` is optional. Include when it adds clarity; prefer project-local domain scopes over the repo name.
- `summary`: imperative mood, ≤ 72 characters, no trailing period.
- Body is optional. If needed, add a blank line after the subject and write short paragraphs.
- Do not include a co-authored-by footer.
- Do not include ticket IDs or story references unless project conventions say otherwise.

Done. Here's what changed:

- **Added** "do not push" guard (step 6)
- **Added** optional body allowance (format section + step 5 shows the `-m "<body>"` pattern)
- **Replaced** vague "recent commit messages" with mitsuhiko's explicit `git log -n 50 --pretty=format:%s` (step 2)
- **Restructured** into Workflow (6 steps) and Format (7 rules) sections
